FT_IC1.evidence: Study uses Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort in the Netherlands, not the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Methods section explicitly states "This study was embedded in the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) Study (www.abcd-study.nl). ABCD is a community-based prospective cohort study" in the Netherlands.

notes: Wrong ABCD cohort (Dutch, not U.S.). Excluded at FT-IC1.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Methods state: "The Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study (ABCD) is a multi-site, longitudinal neuroimaging study following 9-10 year-old youth...ABCD participants with usable structural imaging and SMA data (N = 4277 of 4524)." First data release (2018) from U.S. ABCD.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Group Factor Analysis (GFA) to identify latent variables relating screen media activity to brain structure (cortical thickness, sulcal depth, gray matter volume). Generalized linear mixed-effect models used to investigate associations between SMA and psychopathology/cognition.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in NeuroImage, October 2018, Vol. 185, pp. 140-153. No indication of preprint duplication or prior publication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) measured as IV from youth self-report (watching TV/videos, playing video games, using social media). Paired with individual-trait-level neural outcomes: cortical thickness, sulcal depth, gray matter volume (brain structure); and behavioral/cognitive outcomes: psychopathology (internalizing/externalizing), fluid and crystallized intelligence.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported throughout paper. Mixed-effects model analyses report beta coefficients with CI and p-values for associations between SMA and structural brain indices and outcomes. Cohen's d effect sizes reported for associations with psychopathology (ES 0.06-0.1 for externalizing) and cognition (ES 0.08-0.1 for crystallized, 0.04-0.09 for fluid intelligence).

FT_IC4.evidence: Four SMA-related GFAs explained 37% of variance between SMA and structural brain indices. Generalized linear mixed-effect models show associations between SMA factors and internalizing/externalizing psychopathology and intelligence. Variance explained and effect sizes reported in all analyses.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design. EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies with SM as DV-only; this paper's design does not qualify.
notes: Meets all inclusion criteria. Cross-sectional analysis of SMA as IV predicting neural and behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper describes mobile and wearable technologies and novel data collection methods for the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Abstract states: "In the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, there is great potential for harnessing the acceptability, accessibility, and functionality of mobile and social technologies for in-vivo data capture." Discusses U.S. ABCD with 21 sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a resource/methods paper, not an original empirical analysis. Paper discusses "advances in mobile and wearable technologies and methods of analysis" and describes current/future use of technologies in ABCD (e.g., iPads, accelerometers, passive sensing) but does not report original data analysis results. Explicitly described as outlines of "mobile and social technologies and methods that are currently being used with children and families...and those that will be implemented."

notes: Resource/methods paper describing mobile and wearable technologies for ABCD data collection, not empirical analysis. Excluded at FT-IC5.
